summary: Fowler draws a parallel between craftsmanship in carpentry and software development, using his wife Cindy's appreciation for fine woodworking details as a lens. He discovers a small but thoughtful detail in Apple's iTunes: the software correctly sorts 'Piano Sonata No.8' before 'Piano Sonata No.10' using natural number ordering rather than alphabetical sorting. This seemingly trivial feature required deliberate effort from a developer who cared about the user experience. Fowler expresses appreciation for this invisible craftsmanship.
key_points: Good craftsmanship often manifests in details that most people never notice
Natural sorting (8 before 10) rather than alphabetical sorting (10 before 8) requires deliberate implementation effort
Software quality includes small touches that 'don't look like much, but are actually quite tricky to do right'
Appreciation for craft extends across disciplines from carpentry to software
money_quotes: She particularly appreciates things that don't look like much, but are actually quite tricky to do right.
Someone, somewhere cared enough to make that happen - I appreciate it.
key_insight: True craftsmanship in software reveals itself in the small, invisible details that someone cared enough to get right, even when most users will never notice.
